reasoning:
```json
{
  "label": "The Dark Theater Fund",
  "design_principles": "Optimize for working-life stability first; retirement is overflow. Income gaps are 'intermissions,' not failures. Access funds when needed, penalize nothing.",
  "core_mechanism": "A unified fund accessible during working-year crises, with unused balance converting to retirement income. Survival during earning years takes priority over post-work accumulation.",
  "how_it_works": "Contributions go to a Dark Theater Fund (DTF), not a retirement account. During certified 'dark periods' (involuntary income loss, caregiving, health crisis), you withdraw freely—no penalty, no guilt. At retirement age, your remaining balance converts to income. If you weathered many dark periods, you'll have less retirement but survived. If you had few, you have abundant retirement. The system doesn't judge which path is better. Certification of dark periods uses income tax data plus simple attestation; abuse triggers repayment obligations, not criminal penalty. Employers, platforms, and clients can contribute directly to workers' DTFs as transaction-linked micro-contributions.",
  "what_is_new": "Inverts retirement's core assumption: instead of saving for after work while struggling during work, save for working-life resilience with retirement as overflow. Eliminates artificial separation between emergency savings and retirement.",
  "why_it_works": "For irregular earners, the crisis isn't retirement—it's surviving between gigs. Solving working-year instability lets people work longer, reduces desperate choices, and builds genuine security. Those who use funds early often need less retirement anyway (they adapt, relocate, or extend careers). The 'theatrical intermission' frame removes shame from income gaps.",
  "why_it_fails": "Repeated dark periods could drain the fund completely, leaving nothing for retirement—creating a new 'successfully survived but now destitute' class. Certifying legitimate dark periods is administratively complex and abuse-prone. May require contributions during earning periods that irregular earners still struggle to make consistently.",
  "medium_term": "Irregular earners report dramatically lower financial anxiety. 'Dark period' becomes a neutral life-stage term. Retirement age becomes meaningless as people work flexibly into their 70s with DTF backup.",
  "long_term_vision": "The rigid binary of 'working' vs. 'retired' dissolves. Life becomes a series of performances and intermissions, all financially supported. People take career risks, creative sabbaticals, caregiving years—knowing the dark periods are funded. Retirement is no longer a cliff but a gradual shift in the performance-to-intermission ratio, unique to each life's final act."
}
```

solution.label: The Dark Theater Fund
solution.design_principles: Optimize for working-life stability first; retirement is overflow. Income gaps are 'intermissions,' not failures. Access funds when needed, penalize nothing.
solution.core_mechanism: A unified fund accessible during working-year crises, with unused balance converting to retirement income. Survival during earning years takes priority over post-work accumulation.
solution.how_it_works: Contributions go to a Dark Theater Fund (DTF), not a retirement account. During certified 'dark periods' (involuntary income loss, caregiving, health crisis), you withdraw freely—no penalty, no guilt. At retirement age, your remaining balance converts to income. If you weathered many dark periods, you'll have less retirement but survived. If you had few, you have abundant retirement. The system doesn't judge which path is better. Certification of dark periods uses income tax data plus simple attestation; abuse triggers repayment obligations, not criminal penalty. Employers, platforms, and clients can contribute directly to workers' DTFs as transaction-linked micro-contributions.
solution.what_is_new: Inverts retirement's core assumption: instead of saving for after work while struggling during work, save for working-life resilience with retirement as overflow. Eliminates artificial separation between emergency savings and retirement.
solution.why_it_works: For irregular earners, the crisis isn't retirement—it's surviving between gigs. Solving working-year instability lets people work longer, reduces desperate choices, and builds genuine security. Those who use funds early often need less retirement anyway (they adapt, relocate, or extend careers). The 'theatrical intermission' frame removes shame from income gaps.
solution.why_it_fails: Repeated dark periods could drain the fund completely, leaving nothing for retirement—creating a new 'successfully survived but now destitute' class. Certifying legitimate dark periods is administratively complex and abuse-prone. May require contributions during earning periods that irregular earners still struggle to make consistently.
solution.medium_term: Irregular earners report dramatically lower financial anxiety. 'Dark period' becomes a neutral life-stage term. Retirement age becomes meaningless as people work flexibly into their 70s with DTF backup.
solution.long_term_vision: The rigid binary of 'working' vs. 'retired' dissolves. Life becomes a series of performances and intermissions, all financially supported. People take career risks, creative sabbaticals, caregiving years—knowing the dark periods are funded. Retirement is no longer a cliff but a gradual shift in the performance-to-intermission ratio, unique to each life's final act.
